fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small to-do list core library built out — basically the same pattern we used for that notes feature a while back, where we kept the data layer totally separate from how it gets called. The idea is developers should be able to create individual to-do items, manage a whole list of them, and save/load that list without writing the same glue code everywhere. Items need a label and some kind of done/not-done flag. We also need to be able to flip that flag back and forth, pull out just the finished ones, and persist the whole thing so it survives app restarts. The entry point should read a command in and print results out — but critically the business logic itself shouldn't care about that I/O stuff at all, keep those concerns apart like we always do. One thing I keep seeing go wrong on other projects is when the storage piece is hardcoded in — make sure that's swappable. Also the default state for a new item when you don't say whether it's done or not should be obvious but let's make sure it's explicit. Output format for each operation needs to match what the test harness expects exactly. Malformed inputs shouldn't blow up the whole thing either. Should be compact, not over-engineered. Reach out if anything's unclear!

One quick follow-up since a couple details came up. For a task item, if it's constructed without the completion flag, it needs to default to false. If that flag is provided at construction time, use it exactly as given, so true stays true and false stays false. In construct mode, the output needs to be exactly two lines and only two lines: first 'text=<the task text>', then 'completed=<true or false>'. No extra whitespace and no extra blank lines.

On serialization, this needs to stay symmetric. If we decode a JSON map into a task item and then encode it again, it should come back as a value-equal map with exactly the keys 'text' and 'completed' in that order, where 'text' is a string and 'completed' is a boolean. No extra keys and nothing missing. For serialize mode specifically, print exactly three lines: 'text=<value>', 'completed=<true|false>', then 'json={"text":"<value>","completed":<true|false>}'. That JSON needs the keys in the order text first, then completed, with no extra spaces.

Also for toggle mode, print the starting state as 'initial=<true|false>', then one line per toggle as 'after_<N>=<true|false>' with N starting at 1 and increasing from there. How many times to toggle comes from the 'times' field in the command input.

And just to restate the structure piece a little more plainly, the pattern I was referring to is Separation of Concerns / SOLID: the execution adapter is the main entry point that reads JSON commands from stdin, and that should stay physically and logically separate from the core domain models (TaskItem, TaskCollection) and the persistence abstraction (StorageInterface). Last thing, if you're running the suite directly, it's via 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts '--cases-dir <subdir>' and defaults to test_cases. For each case it writes the raw stdout of the program to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt', and those outputs are namespaced by cases-dir so runs in different dirs don't overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: how is the collection printed after appending
what does the add mode output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The add mode prints 'count=<N>' on the first line, then one line per task in insertion order formatted as '<index>=<text>' where index starts at 1.

The filter_completed mode prints 'count=<N>' where N is the number of completed items, then each completed item's text in original relative order as '<index>=<text>' starting from index 1. Items not completed are excluded entirely.

trigger_keywords: what does the persist mode print
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The persist mode saves to a durable key-value store and then reloads into a fresh empty collection. The output prints 'count=<N>' followed by each reloaded item as '<index>=<text>|<true|false>' where the pipe character separates the text and the completion state. Index starts at 1.

trigger_keywords: can the store be swapped out
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The durable storage concern must sit behind an abstraction (e.g., an interface or abstract class) so the concrete store implementation can be substituted. The collection model must depend on this abstraction, not on a concrete I/O class directly.

trigger_keywords: what happens when the command is invalid
how should bad JSON or unknown mode be handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Malformed or unrecognized commands must be caught and rendered as a neutral error line to stdout — something like 'error=<description>' — without leaking any host-language runtime exception details or stack traces.

The item model, the collection model, the persistence/storage abstraction, and the execution adapter (entry point that reads commands and prints results) must each be in clearly separate units/files. The adapter reads stdin JSON and delegates to the core; the core must not read stdin or write stdout itself.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The durable key-value store used for persistence in Feature 2.3 must be hidden behind an abstraction (interface/abstract class). The TaskCollection depends on this abstraction, not a concrete implementation, so it can be substituted — e.g., an in-memory store for tests and a file-based store in production.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have more detail on that specific point — you'll want to look at the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md for the exact contract. The test cases are the source of truth for expected output format.

feature1_1_construct_task.json.description: Create a single task from its text. When the completion flag is omitted it defaults to not-completed; when supplied it is honored verbatim. The output echoes the stored text and the resolved completion state.

feature1_2_serialize_task.json.description: Round-trip a task through its JSON map representation: decode a map with text and completion keys into a task, then re-encode it. The decoded text and flag are reported, plus the canonical re-encoded JSON which must be value-equal to the original map.

feature1_3_toggle_task.json.description: Toggle a task's completion flag a number of times starting from a known initial state. Each toggle flips the flag, so the reported sequence of states must strictly alternate starting from the initial value.

feature2_1_add_tasks.json.description: Append a sequence of task texts to an initially empty list. The list grows by one per append and preserves insertion order. The output reports the final size followed by each stored text in order.

feature2_2_filter_completed.json.description: From a mixed list of completed and not-completed tasks, select only the completed ones while preserving their original relative order. The output reports the number of completed tasks followed by their texts in order.

feature2_3_persist_roundtrip.json.description: Persist a list of tasks to durable key-value storage, then reload them into a fresh, empty list. The reloaded list must contain exactly the same tasks, in order, with text and completion state preserved through the save/load round-trip.
